data.title: HEQ: Discount Remains Wide For This Diversified Fund
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

John Hancock Diversified Income Fund (HEQ) changed its name from John Hancock Hedged Equity & Income Fund late last year. Along with the name change came an investment policy change as well, which we outlined in our prior update. The main shift was that instead of it being more specifically an equity-focused fund that included hedging through the use of options, it shifted to include more fixed-income exposure while dropping the options.

However, when we last gave the fund a look, we also noted that the fund did not employ any options during the last annual report anyway. Instead, they appeared to implement futures and forward foreign currency contracts as hedging vehicles.

One thing that has remained the same for this fund is that the fund's discount has remained quite wide. In fact, it has hardly budged since our last update and sits at a double-digit-wide discount. This keeps it remaining at a historically wide discount, but investors have fairly consistently kept it at this wider level since the 2023 distribution cut. That makes it an interesting candidate to consider for an investor on the hunt for a flexible hybrid fund, but it's not necessarily a screaming buy, given this could be the new 'normal' trading discount level.

HEQ Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.54.
  • Discount/Premium: -11.26%.
  • Distribution Yield: 9.33%.
  • Expense Ratio: 1.26%.
  • Leverage: N/A.
  • Managed Assets: $146.8 million.
  • Structure: Perpetual.

HEQ's investment objective is to "seek total return consisting of both income and capital gains." The investment objective did not change with the late 2024 changes of the fund.

In an attempt to achieve the objectives, the fund:

Under normal circumstances, the fund will invest at least 25% of its net assets (assets plus borrowings for investment purposes) in equity and equity-related securities and at least 25% in fixed-income securities and fixed-income related instruments. Equity and equity-related securities include common stock, preferred stock, depositary receipts (including American Depositary Receipts and Global Depositary Receipts), index-related securities (including exchange traded funds ("ETFs"), options on equity securities and equity indexes, real estate investment structures (including real estate investment trusts ("REITs")), convertible securities, private placements, convertible preferred stock, rights, warrants, derivatives linked to equity securities or indexes and other similar equity equivalents. Fixed-income related securities may include, but are not limited to, instruments such as: non-investment grade ("high yield" or "junk bond") instruments, swaps, futures, forwards, options, mortgages, and bank loans.

Performance—Discount Remains Wide

Since our prior update, the fund has delivered positive total returns. Though it fell short of the S&P 500 Index during this time, given the fixed-income exposure and continued hedging through derivatives like futures, that isn't all that unexpected. Fixed-income exposure is, in particular, likely one of the main drags in performance due to the substantial gain we've seen in the broader equity market.

Instead, for performance, the fund provides its own relative benchmarks. That includes a Global Aggressive Allocation category, MSCI ACWI, as well as its "Blended Benchmark." Within the blended benchmark is "60% MSCI All Country World Index ("ACWI") and 40% Intercontinental Exchange ("ICE") Bank of America Global High Yield Constrained Index." That helps represent both its global reach and also a heavier allocation to fixed-income exposure these days.

In general, the fund has regularly underperformed all of these relevant benchmarks that it includes. The only time it outperformed was on a net asset value basis with the 5-year mark against its blended index.

That said, we should give a bit more scrutiny to even this before getting too excited. This is because the blended index is heavier toward fixed income, and the 5-year period reflects when fixed income saw significant losses due to aggressive Fed rate hikes. The investment policy to include more fixed income for HEQ started in late 2024, meaning that it was still a heavier equity fund during most of that period.

On the other hand, for the counterargument, one could present the fact that the fund was hedged—and still is incorporating future and forward foreign currency contracts, even now. Hedges come with a cost, and that can often weigh on longer-term performance when the general trend is heading higher for equities.

So while the total returns may not be too impressive relative to their benchmarks, they also aren't necessarily awful when looking at them in isolation. Still, picking up this fund at a wide discount would appear to be the more attractive approach to help potentially juice the upside returns, should the discount ever narrow materially.

The fund is currently trading below its longer-term average level. In fact, at one time, this fund traded at premium levels, but it has been quite some time since.

The last 3-year average discount comes in at around 10%, which makes it less appealing if investors are hoping for a quick narrowing of the discount. With no activist investors in this fund, there doesn't appear to be a clear catalyst for the discount to close soon. There is always the chance of something, but without pressure, managers tend not to have much incentive. There is always a chance that the distribution could be lifted materially, which often generates investor attention—or additionally, announcing a merger with another one of their CEFs that trades at a narrower discount or even a premium is a possibility.

The latter seems like a more viable solution, but they don't necessarily have a similar fund like HEQ to easily fold it into. Instead, it would have to change the focus to more of a preferred fund, such as John Hancock Preferred Income Fund (HPI). With its ~6% premium, that would make it an interesting choice and one that investors would have good reason to vote to approve.

Distribution—Bumped Due To Substantial Discount

The fund currently pays out a quarterly distribution, with a NAV rate of 8.28%, but the market rate is coming in at 9.33%. The bump in the market rate is thanks to the fund trading at a meaningful discount.

Over the long term, the fund has seen quite a bit of a steady trend of NAV declines, which would suggest that the fund has not been able to cover its distribution. For funds with heavy equity allocations, watching the NAV over time is the best and quickest gauge of coverage. This is because the fund will require primarily capital gains to provide coverage for the payout, rather than the income received from the underlying holdings in the form of dividends and interest payments.

For HEQ, the last semi-annual report indicated that net investment income coverage came in at 68%. That's actually on the higher end for a fund that is tilted toward equity, but that makes sense given a shift toward more fixed-income exposure over the last year. In 2024, the fund's NII coverage came in at 52%, for some context.

Of course, even with NAV and share price declines, we saw above that total returns can still be partially respectable, and it doesn't always mean that the fund is producing losses, as the distributions offset the declines.

For tax purposes, the fund's distribution has been characterized mostly as ordinary income in the 2024 year; however, drilling it down further, the fund's $1 paid was $0.457588 in qualified dividends and $0.226004 in non-dividend distribution (aka return of capital). That leaves a relatively smaller portion of $0.316408 as being subject to ordinary income tax rates.

HEQ's Portfolio

During our last update, the fund was still tilted quite heavily toward equity positions at around 70% of the fund. This has come down to around 63% of the fund now, according to CEFConnect, as of September 30, 2025. The fund also lists futures and forward foreign currency contracts as of its latest semi-annual report. Additionally, the fund also has some credit default swaps, but the exposure there is quite minimal. That said, the semi-annual report is for the six months ended June 30, 2025, so it's a bit more dated.

Shifting to the fund's website, they outline the broader asset composition of the portfolio as of October 31, 2025. On that front, we've seen a slight shift to a higher allocation of collateralized mortgage obligations from the ~9% level previously—ABS exposure also shifted a bit higher, but not materially. This would indicate some shift to fixed-income securities; however, with the sector allocation, it doesn't define more specifically what is allocated to equity versus fixed income.

With that said, we can see just how diversified the fund is, as the name would imply. This is then followed through not only on the types of securities the fund holds, but also reflected in the over 550 different positions the fund carries as well. Worth also noting is that the fund carries a heavier exposure to financials compared to the heavier tilt toward technology, which has comprised larger swaths of the S&P 500 Index these days.

Moving to the top ten holdings also further solidifies its 'diversified' name, given that the largest allocation is to JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) at over a 1% allocation, but all the other holdings are sub-1% level—which actually drops quite rapidly with the tenth spot, Philip Morris International Inc. (PM), coming in at a 0.63% weight.

Conclusion

HEQ trades at a deep discount to its net asset value, but this appears to be more of the new level of the fund. They shifted the fund's investment policy and name around late last year. However, that hasn't seemed to make investors any more interested in this relatively smaller fund. While it is an interesting name, and I'm quite fond of John Hancock, the fund sponsor, this is another one of those funds that doesn't seem to have a clear catalyst for closing the discount. Without activist exposure in the fund, the management team doesn't have a lot of incentive to make a meaningful effort to reduce the discount.

data.title: 4 Reasons The Odds Of A Santa Claus Rally Just Went Up Big
data.description:
I have been bearish recently, and the market has taken a hit over the past few days; however, I am getting a bit more positive (at least in the short term) that the chances of a year-end rally are getting much more likely. I have been calling for a pullback, and now that the market is down about 5% from recent highs, there has been a small pullback, which makes the setup going into the end of the year more positive. There are also a few other factors that could make a year-end rally more likely, and one of them is the potential for a rate cut in December. I also believe that President Trump is seeing that consumers are feeling pinched by high prices and that is a motivating factor for him to "juice" the economy before the midterm elections.

We all saw a socialist Democrat win the mayor's race in New York City by running on a platform that promises many free things and even a government-run grocery store, and Democrats won in other key races. I believe this raises the stakes for the Trump Administration to create some policies that could ease the pressure on consumers. The recent economic data that shows deterioration in the job market and declining consumer sentiment is likely putting pressure on the Fed to cut rates, and that pressure probably just increased a bit with the stock market declining by about 5%. The last thing the Fed potentially wants is to see the economy continue to weaken and have the stock market melt down in December, which could further crimp consumer sentiment and the levels of shopping during the big holiday shopping season.

Reason #1: A Key Earnings Report Was A Hurdle That Was Just Cleared

With tech stocks not trading well in recent days, I believed investors needed to get past Nvidia's (NVDA) earnings report and see that this company beat earnings, which is exactly what it did. Clearing this Nvidia earnings hurdle allows the market to move forward, and with the stock up after the earnings report, it is one less major event for investors to worry about. The strong earnings and guidance from Nvidia should be enough to get the tech stock rally back on track.

Reason #2: I Believe A Fed Rate Cut In December Is Coming

There has been a clear softening trend in the jobs data in recent months—whether you look at the government data that was provided before the shutdown or at the more recent private payrolls data, it shows rapid deterioration. Challenger, Gray & Christmas recently announced that 153,074 jobs were cut in October. This represents a 183% increase from September. The number of layoff announcements in 2025 has made it the worst year for announced layoffs since 2009, and that is something that the Fed has to take seriously. Aside from the layoffs that are due to DOGE job cuts in the government sector and some layoffs among many small businesses, potentially due to tariffs, there has also been a new wave of layoffs that are being blamed on AI and automation, which seem to be gaining steam.

A recent report from Bernie Sanders suggests that 100 million jobs could be replaced by AI in the next ten years. This potentially represents about 10 million lost jobs each year on average for the next ten years. This could be a wave of layoffs that we have not seen before, and it is something that the Fed has to start building into its forecasts and policies because if they don't, unemployment could surge. When there is a high level of unemployment, it can put pressure on wages and bonuses paid to those that still have jobs, and that could limit wage growth and consumer spending. Many mega-cap tech companies have started to lay off workers that are being replaced by AI, and some companies are just not hiring new college graduates because jobs that could have gone to a college graduate are now being replaced by AI in some cases. This seems to be creating a new drag on the economy for even middle-class and wealthy families who are being forced to support their adult children who are not finding the first job they were expecting after graduation.

It is clear that the Fed is going to be challenged in getting inflation to drop, which is currently running at about 3%, down to its stated target of 2%. Since the Fed is not going to get rid of inflation anytime soon, they might as well try to win the battle against further job losses by cutting rates as an "insurance policy" against the weakening job market and a potential recession. I don't believe a 1/4 point rate cut will cause a surge in inflation, and the job cuts and lack of hiring we are seeing are very disinflationary and that should appease any of the more hawkish members of the Fed.

I believe the Federal Reserve is not just looking at the jobs market, because consumer sentiment is an important metric when considering the health of the economy, and consumer sentiment recently hit an all-time low—and loan delinquencies are rising. This is worrisome since consumer spending represents about 2/3rds of the U.S. economy. This weak consumer sentiment and increasing delinquencies imply inflationary pressures are likely to be easing, and that should give the Fed room to cut rates in December.

I want to add that even if the Fed does not cut rates in December, it makes it likely that the rate cut will just get delayed slightly, since they have another meeting in January during which they can cut rates. I believe this means that if there is no December rate cut, it won't matter too much, as that just makes a January rate cut more likely, and the Fed is likely to communicate this in December, which would reassure the market and allow for a Santa Claus rally.

Reason #3: President Trump Might Take Action To Boost The Consumer And The Chances For A Year-End Rally

I think the chances of a year-end rally are increasing because the stock market has just taken out a lot of the froth and reset valuations for many stocks, and I also think a Fed rate cut in December is likely to happen and help create a year-end rally. However, I believe that President Trump is also now floating a few ideas that the market could start to focus on that might give investors hope for 2026, and this could also ignite the often hoped-for Santa Claus rally.

As I recently wrote about in this article, President Trump seems to be seeing the weakness in the economy and the pressure facing many consumers, as he recently proposed a $2,000 tariff dividend. In this article I also mentioned that tax refunds are poised to be larger in 2026, and this could boost consumer confidence and spending early next year. Investors could look at these factors as being a source of relief and a reason to be bullish towards what is seasonally a strong time for stocks at the end of each year. President Trump also just recently announced a reduction in tariffs on many major food items in order to take the pressure off of consumers. This could help reduce food inflation concerns that the Fed might have. As I wrote in this article, President Trump recently proposed a 50-year mortgage in order to make housing more affordable. I view this as more of a gimmick than as a real solution, but it does show that President Trump is focusing on boosting the consumer and the economy.

Reason #4: The Stock Market May Have Found Support At This Key Level, Which Sets Up Nicely For A Year-End Rally

The S&P 500 Index (SPY) has recently declined about 5% from the recent highs, and this 5% decline is a level where some investors like to buy the dip. But the other factor is that the recent selloff has put this index back down to around the 50-day moving average, a key level that is shown by the blue trend line on the chart below. This could be an area of support that sets the market up for a year-end rally.

Here's My Year-End Buying Strategy

Amazon (AMZN) shares surged to around $255 after the company reported Q3 earnings, but the stock has lost almost all the gains and just traded for about $220. This appears to be an ideal year-end buying opportunity, and I bought more shares at around $220 and will add more around this level. I am also focusing on beaten-down stocks that are probably being impacted by tax-loss selling at this time of year. This means I am buying stocks like Whirlpool (WHR), Stanley Black & Decker (SWK), Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG), Deckers (DECK), and others that fit this profile. Buying stocks that are being impacted by tax-loss selling can lead to gains in the coming weeks when this tax-loss selling fades.

Where I Could Be Wrong

There are still hawkish comments coming from some Fed members, and if the Fed does not cut rates in December, this could cause a fresh wave of selling that takes the market lower. Some of President Trump's proposals may not happen or may not be enough to move the needle in terms of the economy and the stock market. If more negative economic data is released, it could put more pressure on stock market valuations, even if the Fed cuts rates.

In Summary

I believe President Trump and the Fed are seeing the weakness in the job market and consumer sentiment, and I believe both will respond to this soon, which could fuel a seasonal year-end rally. I am still bearish on the economy and the stock market overall, but I see a potential tradeable rally opportunity at year-end. This is because the market has recently experienced a pullback and also because I believe the Fed could give the market a rate cut that it needs to boost stocks and the economy during the all-important month of December. For this reason, I would put a hold rating on the S&P 500 Index and consider selling it into strength if we see a year-end rally.

data.title: Ginkgo Bioworks: Scaling Science, Not Revenue
data.description:
Ginkgo Bioworks Holdings, Inc.'s (DNA) business remained steady in the third quarter, and guidance suggests this situation will persist until at least the end of the year. While cost-cutting efforts are helping to reduce Ginkgo's cash burn, the company needs to return to growth in order to transition to profitability. There is little reason to be optimistic on this front, as Ginkgo's Tools and Services businesses are well over 12 months old and yet to create a growth resurgence. Ginkgo's Biosecurity business also continues to tread water. Some of this is likely due to macro conditions, but it is hard not to feel that Ginkgo doesn't have its business model figured out and that its value proposition isn't resonating with customers. This is an issue I first raised in early 2022, and Ginkgo's share price is now down over 90% since then.

I previously highlighted how it would be difficult for Ginkgo Bioworks to transition to profitability due to its inability to generate growth. Ginkgo still has a substantial cash balance, but its latest equity raise points towards management caution regarding its financial strength. Even if Ginkgo doesn't need to continue raising capital, investors face the threat of ruinous dilution, due in large part to excessive SBC. As a result, I remain neutral to negative on Ginkgo's prospects until the demand environment improves or Ginkgo can demonstrate that its newer products have broad appeal.

Market Conditions

Ginkgo continues to face a challenging demand environment, which largely stems from the rapid rise in interest rates in 2022. Funding for academic customers is also under pressure currently, although this doesn't appear to be a particularly important vertical for Ginkgo. While biopharma spend has been soft in recent years, this customer segment has actually been one of the few bright spots for Ginkgo. The company's industrial and consumer businesses appear to have come to almost a complete halt, though.

Ginkgo Bioworks Business Updates

Ginkgo's Cell Engineering business continues to shift towards projects that are smaller in scope, driven by its automation, AI, and datapoints products. While Ginkgo's Research Solutions remain the core of the business, it has been deemphasized somewhat in recent years.

Ginkgo had 102 revenue-generating Cell Engineering programs in the third quarter, a decline of 5% YoY, with Ginkgo suggesting that this was due to program rationalization. The decline is concerning, though, given Ginkgo's shift in focus towards programs that are smaller in scope. There is an ongoing transition towards biopharma and government customers, with demand from ag customers also remaining steady.

Recent program wins include a $22 million monoclonal antibody manufacturing project that was awarded by BARDA. This project aims to reduce the cost of domestically manufacturing antibodies. The project involves a number of parties, though, and the $22 million figure is a maximum contract value, so it is unclear how much revenue Ginkgo will receive. Ginkgo previously partnered with Antheia on the domestic production of active pharmaceutical ingredients. While Antheia's business continues to progress, there haven't been any partnership updates since the initial announcement.

Ginkgo and Bayer also recently extended their agriculture partnership, which primarily relates to engineering microbes. This includes nitrogen-fixing biologicals, which could be extremely valuable if successful. Ginkgo has been working on this since at least 2021, and it is not clear how much progress has been made or how close a product is to market. Pivot Bio already has a nitrogen-fixing bacteria on the market for corn. This is a supplement to synthetic fertilizer and manure rather than a replacement. As a private company, limited information is available about Pivot Bio and its financial performance. The company generated over $100 million in revenue in 2023, up around 60% from the prior year. While this is a positive in terms of end-market potential, it is concerning that Ginkgo isn't leading, as its scale and automation were supposed to enable it to dominate these types of opportunities.

Ginkgo's newer offerings include Datapoints, Automation, and Reagents. While these solutions were designed to drive adoption of Ginkgo's platform, it isn't clear what financial impact they are having. Progress appears modest given that these products have been on the market for around 18 months and Ginkgo's Cell Engineering revenue has been fairly flat over that time frame. The company's revenue mix in 2030 is expected to be something like 80-20 tools versus services, which indicates a dramatic shift in mix is expected, driven by the rapid growth of the Tools business.

Financial Analysis

Cell Engineering revenue in Q3 was 29 million USD, down 61% YoY. This isn’t as bad as it appears, as the third quarter of 2024 included $45 million of non-cash revenue, which was related to the termination of Ginkgo’s agreement with Motif Foodworks, a relationship that didn’t yield any real value for either party (Motif went bankrupt). Interestingly, legal troubles were part of Motif's downfall, which relates to claims that it infringed on the intellectual property of Impossible Foods. There is also evidence that Cronos Group ran into similar issues and was forced to license IP from Aurora and 22nd Century Group as a result. Biosecurity revenue totaled $9 million in the third quarter at a 19% gross margin (excludes SBC).

Ginkgo is now guiding to $167-$187 million in revenue in 2025, with $117-$137 million in Cell Engineering revenue and upwards of $40 million in Biosecurity revenue. This guidance suggests modest sequential growth in Q4 but a further YoY decline.

Ginkgo's adjusted EBITDA loss in the third quarter was $56 million. This is somewhat deceptive due to a $21 million shortfall related to Ginkgo's AI partnership with Google Cloud. This contract has now been amended, reducing future commitments and extending the relevant period. This was initially a 5-year partnership that was announced in the middle of 2023. It is worth keeping in mind that there was a lot of buzz around this partnership when it was announced, with investors seemingly not recognizing that Ginkgo was signing up for services from Google. Rather than creating an inflection point in Ginkgo's business or indicating a strategic interest from Google, the relationship has resulted in unnecessary expenses.

Cash burn in Q3 totaled $28 million, with the YoY reduction driven by Ginkgo's restructuring efforts. While Ginkgo still has work to do, its business is rapidly becoming viable from a cash burn perspective. It may be difficult for Ginkgo to cut its cash costs much further, which is partly due to unused real estate that the company is currently stuck with.

The shift in the nature of Ginkgo's business over the past few years also means that losses are now less acceptable. Ginkgo's previous focus on participating in downstream value meant that there was a mismatch between when costs were incurred and revenue recognized. This timing discrepancy no longer exists, though. The shift presumably should also mean that more Cell Engineering revenue is being recognized upfront, making the lack of growth even more damning.

Ginkgo had around $462 million in cash and cash equivalents at the end of the third quarter, which is a relatively large balance relative to the current burn rate. Despite this, Ginkgo recently entered into a $100 million equity sales deal, suggesting the company believes it will continue to burn cash many years into the future.

Conclusion

There is not much to like about Ginkgo's business at the moment, and the narrative supporting the stock continues to weaken. While Ginkgo's attempt to become the AWS of biology has largely been forgotten, it is worthwhile keeping in mind that there don't appear to have been any meaningfully successful commercial products to come out of the platform. This doesn't paint the capabilities of the company in the best light and suggests a reason that Ginkgo has struggled to build demand.

Considerable blame for the current situation can likely be placed on macro headwinds, but biopharma sentiment appears to have improved in recent months, and Ginkgo hasn't noted any boost from this. This stands in contrast to the likes of Twist Bioscience Corporation (TWST), with Twist quite positive on the near-term prospects of its Biopharma segment due to demand from AI-enabled drug discovery customers. Ginkgo should be exposed to this tailwind as it has invested in antibody developability capabilities.

Much of Ginkgo's value proposition now appears to be based around assets acquired from Zymergen, with Ginkgo's Foundry business reduced to a CRO. As a commodity supplier of lab services offering modest growth, Ginkgo should probably trade on a lower revenue multiple than it currently does, particularly when considering cash burn and dilution. As a result, I expect continued losses and weak growth to further pressure the share price.

data.title: Solana Attempts Rebound As Buyers Defend Key Channel Support (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya​

Solana (SOL-USD) attempted to stabilize near the mid-$140 region on Thursday after one of its steepest multi-week declines of the year pushed the token below the lower boundary of its long-running ascending channel. The move follows a drop from the $220 zone driven by heavy market-wide selling and persistent outflows across major spot venues.

The latest bounce is the first sign that buyers are attempting to defend structure rather than absorb forced selling. But the backdrop remains tense, with Solana still trading below several key moving averages and carrying the weight of months of distribution.

Technical trend weakens as Solana stays below major EMAs

The chart shows Solana trading beneath the 20-, 50-, 100- and 200-day EMAs, now clustered between $153 and $182, forming a thick overhead resistance band. The early November break below the ascending channel signaled a transition from a trending advance to a corrective phase. Recent attempts to retest resistance have stalled near the Supertrend line around $172, which remains firmly bearish.

Solana is yet to reclaim even the 20-day EMA. Until the token closes back inside the channel or breaks above the initial resistance layer near $153, the broader structure retains a clear downward bias. The recent rebound from $128—aligned with the channel’s lower boundary—offered a technical foothold, but buyers still face significant work to reverse the trend.

RSI readings across the daily and 4H charts reflect neutral-to-soft momentum conditions. The indicator has struggled to break above the mid-line, underscoring that any strength so far has been reactive rather than driven by conviction.

Flow data shows persistent selling as spot outflows continue

Spot flows remain one of the dominant bearish forces. Solana posted another net outflow of roughly $3M on November 20, extending a multi-month sequence of red prints that began in mid-July. The flow profile shows heavy distribution even during relief rallies, with inflows rarely matching the magnitude of outflows.

This pattern raises questions about the durability of support zones. When rebounds are fueled by short covering or tactical buying instead of long-term accumulation, the base becomes more fragile. The heavy outflow history has closely mirrored Solana’s fall from its $220 highs.

Derivatives positioning, however, offers a more mixed picture. Open interest has climbed more than 4 percent in the past 24 hours to above $7.5B, and volume has surged over 20 percent. Long-short ratios are modestly skewed toward buyers on Binance and OKX, with some top-trader positioning showing more than 2:1 long exposure. But the liquidation tape shows short-side liquidations outweighing long wipeouts, signaling that the bounce may be fueled partially by short unwinds rather than clean demand.

This combination—rising open interest, uneven liquidations and increasing volume—often precedes a larger directional move, though not necessarily a sustained reversal.

Key levels define the path ahead as volatility tightens

The $130–$140 region remains the immediate battleground. Solana’s low near $128 aligned almost perfectly with long-term support, giving buyers a natural area to defend. Holding this level allows the token to grind toward $153 and potentially retest the $172 zone where the broader trend flipped bearish.

A decisive close below $130 exposes Solana to a deeper retreat toward $115, with little structural support until that area. The upward path is equally clear: reclaiming the 20-day EMA and breaking above the mid-channel dashed trendline would be the first signals strong enough to suggest the downtrend is losing grip.

For now, Solana remains in a consolidation defined by heavy outflows, cautious derivatives participation, and a long-term trendline break that still weighs on sentiment. Buyers are selective, not absent, leaving the market searching for a catalyst capable of restoring momentum.

In earlier coverage, we highlighted that Solana’s trendline break in early November would pull the token into a deeper corrective phase unless it reclaimed the 20-day EMA. The current setup reinforces that expectation, with price still struggling beneath the EMA cluster and spot flows continuing to show distribution.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: September Jobs Report: Mixed Signals Make The Fed's December Decision Difficult
data.description:
The long-awaited September jobs report landed this morning, giving the Fed its only formal read on labor market conditions before the December meeting. The release offered a mixed picture, with one survey pointing to a late-quarter rebound in hiring and the other raising fresh concerns about rising unemployment. That contrast raises the stakes for policymakers, who must judge whether the labor market is genuinely weakening or simply sending noisy signals at a moment when they have little other data to lean on. With no October report and a delayed November release, today’s data carries outsized weight in shaping the Fed’s next move.

Establishment Survey

U.S. nonfarm payroll employment increased by 119,000 in September, a surprise burst of strength exhibited by the labor market at the end of Q3. Not only was this increase above consensus expectations of a 50,000 increase, it was the largest increase since April and the third largest increase so far this year (just below March's gain of 120,000). Most significantly, the strong growth in September reverses a string of weak readings that began in May and lasted through the summer and resulted in job growth averaging around 18,500 over the course of those four months. Revisions in today’s report made those numbers look even worse as the July total was taken down -7,000 to 72,000, and the August total was revised down -26,000, such that employment fell -2,000 during the month.

The headline number was supported by the strongest private payroll growth since April. The 97,000 jobs added by private sector firms were ahead of expectations of a 62,000 increase and substantially above the September ADP private sector employment change estimate of -29,000. As usual, the service sector provided most of the job growth, adding 87,000, while the goods sector only added 10,000. Two service industries specifically accounted for almost all the increase in nonfarm payrolls: education and health services (+59,000) and leisure and hospitality (+47,000). Job growth in other industries was a wash. Notably, with the Trump admin’s focus on tariffs in mind, manufacturing employment fell for the 5th straight month since the reciprocal tariffs were put in place in April (down -58,000 cumulatively since then).

Government job growth made a small positive contribution at +22,000. This included federal employment down -3,000, offset by 25,000 jobs added in state & local governments. This, of course, doesn’t include the job losses that may have come about as a result of the reduction-in-force orders during the government shutdown. Jobless claims data suggest that the number was not insignificant. Initial claims by federal employees totaled 5,719 in the week of November 8th, well above 615 in the same week a year ago.

Taken as given, the data from the Establishment survey looked pretty solid. Hiring appears to have bounced back after a weak summer, and the private sector accounted for a majority of the increase. The breadth of job gains across industries was not great, but there didn’t appear to be evidence of widespread layoffs. The one caveat is that these numbers are likely to be revised, and revisions have not been friendly.

Household Survey

If you were optimistic reading through the Establishment survey data, this is the part of the report where things get ugly. The headline unemployment rate saw a significant increase in September, rising 13 bps to 4.44%, above expectations of it remaining at 4.3% and the highest since October 2021. This increase wasn’t a matter of rounding. The print came within 1.1 bps of rounding up to 4.5%. It sounds nitpicky to comment at this level of detail, but it is important to note these things since a majority of people view these data points as a single data point. The takeaway is that there was a solid, measurable increase in unemployment in September, confirming a clear trend that has been established this year.

The increase in the unemployment rate came from an increase in the total number of unemployed (+219,000) that resulted from a sizeable increase in the civilian labor force (+470,000). While there was an increase in the employed population (+251,000) and the employment rate (+0.1 ppts to 59.7%), the raw increase in the unemployed was just too high relative to the increase in the number of employed. Interestingly, this does contradict some Fed officials’ views on the labor market that suggest labor supply and labor demand are both going down, leading to equilibrium rather than slack.

The age breakdown shows that prime-age and older workers drove the entire increase in September’s unemployment rate. The youngest cohort, 16 to 19 years, actually saw a notable decline in their jobless rate to 13.2%, and workers aged 20 to 24 held steady at 9.2%. With younger groups either improving or unchanged, the pressure came from older segments. The prime age unemployment rate rose 0.1 ppts to 3.5%, the 55 and over rate jumped 0.4 ppts to 3.3%, and early prime age workers posted the sharpest move with a 0.4 ppt increase to 4.8%. This pattern points to a weaker labor market because job losses are increasingly concentrated among core working-age adults rather than temporary or seasonal young workers, signaling more persistent underlying softness rather than noise in the data.

The Household survey data make it clear that unemployment moved meaningfully higher in September. On top of that, the increase in the labor force and in the participation rate push back against the notion that the labor market is in a sort of equilibrium state where labor supply and labor demand are both falling in tandem, such that there was nothing more than a slow cooling in the labor market.

Market Reaction

The September jobs report is almost two months old, but old data is all that the market and the Fed have to feed on for now (not including alternate data sources). On balance, investors have assessed that the September data showed a further weakening in the labor market, and thus, assets are broadly trading in a dovish manner.

  • S&P futures, which have suffered recently from weakening December rate cut expectations, bounced following the release of the report and sustained an uptrend into the official market open. About an hour into the US session, the major indexes are posting solid gains, with the S&P 500 up 1.7% and the NASDAQ up 1.9%. Nvidia’s strong earnings may be helping, but the 1.2% advance in the equal-weighted S&P 500 fund, Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP) suggests that broad-based buying is underway and that a dovish impulse is likely the main driver of today’s equity strength.
  • Another reason to believe that the market is being fueled by dovishness is that Treasury rates saw a broad-based move lower after the jobs report was released. Across most durations, the decline is between -2 to -3 bps, and those moves have been more or less sustained in the two hours of trading following the release. If the downward move is extended, it could put short-term rates at or near the lows of the month.
  • The dollar has had more of a choppy morning. Going into the jobs report release, the U.S. dollar index (DXY) increased before falling sharply after the report came out. The declines since then have been mostly sustained, and DXY is about even on the day. Against major currencies, the moves are more mixed: down -0.3% against the UK pound, up 0.5% against the Japanese yen, and roughly flat against the euro.

The most important question that the market will want answered is how the data today will impact the Fed. We saw in the October FOMC minutes some notes that implied the hawks slightly outnumbered the doves, tipping the balance slightly towards a December pause. CME’s FedWatch put a pause probability at about 70% at the end of trading yesterday. The data today, considered more dovish than hawkish by the market, has brought the probability of a cut down to just over 58%.

Here is why I think the Fed will still pause in December. Even with this report leaning soft, it is unlikely to push officials toward a cut. The data is old, lagging by nearly two months, and the underlying details are too mixed to offer a clear read on sustained deterioration. With no October report and a delayed November release, policymakers will head into the meeting with less information than usual, and Chair Powell has indicated that limited visibility tends to make him more inclined to pause out of caution. Recent Fed communication has also leaned more heavily on inflation concerns, suggesting that price stability still outweighs pockets of labor market weakness. It will be a close call, but the overall backdrop still favors another pause rather than an early pivot.

data.title: September Added Jobs, But Raised The Unemployment Rate
data.description:
The September jobs report from the BLS was delayed until Nov. 20th, due to the government shutdown. While this report was released in full, we've gotten news that the October report will be released incomplete and partial. That report may not report on the unemployment rate at all. We should get back to the standard reporting in December.

Don't worry, we'll be fully back on track by mid-December. Here is the schedule for delayed reporting for those interested.

The September report came in above expectations, which is typically considered a good thing, though I am skeptical of the current figure given ongoing trends and the past revisions that the BLS has done to prints of this magnitude, where they get slimmer over the next two months of releases while the survey data continues to roll in.

This is because, while we added 119,000 jobs, the expectation was set at 55,000. This discrepancy is huge, and often leads to revisions in the following months, usually down. Remember that those negative months in June and August were revised down that low; they initially came in positive, looking like we added jobs.

I don't want to pour water on the report, or be sour on good news like doubled expectations in jobs added, but it came at the same time that they are reporting rising unemployment, which signals to me that we are likely overestimating for September.

Unemployment Rises

Despite the doubling of the initial estimate, the unemployment rate is still rising, now officially 4.4% in September, higher than I expected. This signals a breakout of the range that it has been stuck in all year.

Of course, this is not the full picture of unemployment, as I recently covered in "The Real Unemployment Rate is Obscured and Rising" earlier this month. This is the U-3 metric, which is imperfect, but does offer us an easy benchmark to read and evaluate the economy. Just know that this isn't the full unemployment picture.

The scorecard for jobs looks much like the rest of them from this year: we added mostly healthcare jobs at the expense of professional services and manufacturing. This split between which sectors are gaining jobs and which are losing is nothing new. It's just a continuation of the trend I've been calling, "All the jobs are healthcare jobs."

This is not a new trend. It's accelerating this year, but it's been a long burn. See this map of the change in top employers for every state; what used to be mostly manufacturing and retail employers have, by and large, given way to healthcare firms which now dominate 38 states.

Alternative Jobs Data

One of the most helpful things to remember about this report is that it's old. We already know a bit about what happened after this report, which is part of why its publication had little effect on the market (which is responding to Nvidia's latest earnings beat).

During the government shutdown and the subsequent data blackout from the BLS, alternative data sources started popping up everywhere. Monthly publications moved to weekly to cover gaps in the blackout, and others released paid reports for free. This data isn't as keen as that from the BLS, and is noisier in general, but is useful in figuring out where we're going from September while we have to wait for data to catch up.

Chicago Fed Nowcast

The Chicago Fed provides both separation and hiring data, which allows us to compile their own nowcast of the unemployment rate, which they have at 4.3%, signaling that they believe the rate will come back down next month.

Despite this, they are still showing both an expected rise in separations and expected fall in hirings from unemployment, confirming that we've bucked the trend from this year and are still breaking into new territory.

The Challenger Report

Every month, Challenger, Gray, & Christmas (CG&C) produce their labor market report. This report is the most noisy that we have, because it tracks job cut announcements instead of unemployed people. This means that it tends to respond to extreme conditions very well, and that it tends to overestimate.

Not all announced job cuts lead to actual job cuts, and so that skews data. Not every point increase in this figure counts as a full job lost when it's reported by CG&C. This doesn't make the data useless, as it's still helpful in showing trends, but don't get too hung up on the actual figures.

Still, it's concerning, and this figure corroborates the fear I have of jobs being revised down in this September report. The figure today is higher than that of any year since 2020 and, before that, 2008-2009.

Companies don't announce layoffs like this in good times, right? Maybe it's AI after all. That's why we're seeing the huge drop in professional services roles, also the kind to be announced as layoffs and counted in this data set.

The Fed Is Still Stuck

The market is still unclear on how this round of macro data will impact the Fed, with the odds swinging back toward 50/50 again of a cut or hold in the Fed Funds Rate at the December FOMC meeting.

This is because the Fed is stuck in an economic "rock and hard place," where they are being asked to deal with both inflation and unemployment both moving up at the same time.

This is just showing the positioning of current futures traders, so we have a lot of time for this to change, but for now, I am in the camp of the left column, believing that we will see a cut in December.

This is because the unemployment rate is accelerating faster than inflation, and so will need to be dealt with first. However, I have been shaken out of my stance that we will get any more cuts past this one in December until May when we get a new appointee for the chair position.

I expect that language around this cut to be laden with hedging verbiage like we've been seeing, if not more overt, from the Fed. In the last meeting, Powell described the cut as a "risk management cut," and said that there is "no risk-free path" for interest rates currently. To me, that is a signal that we are being prepared for them to allow one of these figures to run hotter than they'd like, and I expect that to be inflation over the labor market if they had to pick.

While this may not be a popular choice for people who already have jobs and who may have a myopic view of the labor market, and thus are just focused on how inflation affects them personally, I expect it to be an overall positive for the economy for rates to come down and the labor market to be focused on over inflation.

Conclusion

Ultimately, the Fed still has to make a choice between jobs and inflation, even with this positive report from September. That's exactly it, though, that the report is old and so the market has already moved on despite the surprise in both the amount of jobs added, but also the increased unemployment rate.

This print makes me confident that we will see a cut in December, despite the market pricing in a hold currently. We still have time, and a few more data releases, before the next interest rate decision in December. Things may change by then.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: HeartFlow: Imagining Better Days
data.description:
When HeartFlow (HTFL) went public in August, I concluded that investors were putting their heart in. Its non-invasive CAD heart diagnostic platform and imaging therapies address a large and growing market, as HeartFlow has seen continued sales momentum in the second and third quarters. However, these revenue gains came with little operating leverage, which has been stagnating somewhat.

This relative margin stagnation and either slower future revenue growth (or a conservative guidance) make me hard-pressed to see real appeal just yet, but keenly interested to keep a close eye on the shares here.

Tackling Heart Disease

HeartFlow is based on the observation that heart disease is the leading cause of death in the US, responsible for one in every five deaths taking place. There are some 13 million people in the US alone with coronary artery disease, with a heart attack taking place every forty seconds, resulting in hundreds of billions in healthcare costs incurred each year.

HeartFlow was based on precise measurement of CAD testing. This increased CAD detection has real advantages, among others avoiding unnecessary testing, this being a non-invasive test, while allowing for improved medical management as well.

By now, its solutions have been used to help some 400,000 patients, with over 99% of US covered lives included. This is driven by HeartFlow's personalized 3D Model to provide actionable insights into plaque volume, blood flow, and stenosis, overcoming limitations of traditional non-invasive imaging tests relating to both false positives and negatives.

In essence, this allows us to better visualize plaque accumulating on the walls of coronary arteries, with reduced blood flow increasing the chances of a heart attack or stroke, after plaque builds up because of fat, cholesterol, and calcium. Targeting a $5 billion addressable market, such non-invasive tests are needed but are expensive at hundreds or thousands of dollars per test.

Back to IPO Day

HeartFlow went public at $19 per share in August, priced one and a half dollars above the midpoint of the preliminary offering range, levels at which the company was valued at around one and a half billion, however, including a net cash position of more than a quarter of a billion.

Revenues rose by 44% to $126 million in the year 2024, as operating losses narrowed from $73 million in 2023 to $61 million. Growth continued into 2025, with first-quarter sales up 39% to $37 million, trending around $150 million per annum. However, operating losses of $17.5 million were actually up in dollar terms compared to the same period before.

Second quarter sales were seen up 39% to $43 million but ended up rising 40% to $43 million and change, with operating losses reported around $15 million. While the annualised revenue run rate of around $170 million looked solid in relation to a $1.2 billion enterprise valuation, the company was still incurring large losses. All this changed a bit as shares rose to the $29 mark on the first day of trading, pushing up operating asset valuations to $2.1 billion.

At that valuation, HeartFlow was valued at 12 times annualized sales; however, loss-making sales here, as I failed to have conviction given this backdrop, also given the risks of a one-product business, losses reported, stiff competition, and changes in the payer mix, etc.

Struggling

In September, HeartFlow announced that it obtained US FDA clearance for its next-generation plaque analysis algorithm, with this also being covered by Cigna across Commercial and Medicare Advantage plans from October.

In November, the company reported a 41% increase in third-quarter sales to $46.3 million, with GAAP operating losses pretty flat at $15.1 million. This came in at par compared to the year before, as R&D intensity was increasing on a relative basis, with its costs reported at $17.9 billion.

Trading at $28, an 81 million share count results in a nearly $2.3 billion equity valuation, or around $2.0 billion if we factor in net cash holdings of $291 million. Based on the annualised sales run rate close to $180 million, sales multiples have come in a bit at 11 times, but these remain loss-making sales. The company guided for full-year sales between $173.0 and $173.5 million. This suggests a $46.1-$46.6 million fourth quarter sales outlook, in line with the third quarter.

This is somewhat of a damper, but could easily be conservative, as is often the case with businesses that recently went public, often tending to guide conservatively. Moreover, I like the progress of the business, as this is a very interesting proposition, with potential for a take-out as well. After all, the innovative technology and strong growth, make this a potential M&A target as well.

Given all this, I am quite upbeat on the prospects for the business here, as shares have come down from intermediate highs around $40 per share. Right now, 11 times annualised sales multiple looks within reason given that growth is seen around 40% here, although some greater operating leverage would be welcomed. That, however, is not badly needed, given the net cash balances employed, sufficient to finance current loss rates for another 4–5 years at this rate.

In between all this, I am not yet willing to get involved at current levels, although I am happy to get involved at a high single-digit sales multiple here, as I look forward to keeping a close eye on the business, but right now I cannot commit my heart to the proposition here just yet.

data.title: UDOW: Benefits And Risks Of The 3x Leveraged Dow Jones ETF
data.description:
Fast facts about the ProShares UltraPro Dow30 ETF

The ProShares UltraPro Dow30 ETF (UDOW) is a leveraged ETF based on the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index with a factor of 3x. UDOW was launched on February 9, 2010, has a net expense ratio of 0.95%, and a trailing 12-month yield of 1.31%. It is a mid-size but very liquid ETF, with $656 million of AUM (assets under management) and an average daily dollar volume of $270 million. It means over 40% of AUM changes hands on average in a trading day, pointing to the fund’s popularity among short-term traders. The issuer, ProShares, specializes in leveraged and inverse ETFs, with about one hundred of these types of products based on equity indexes, bond indexes, commodities, currencies and cryptos.

Strategy

UDOW has the objective to provide, before fees and expenses, three times the daily performance of the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index, which is also the underlying index of SPDR® Dow Jones Industrial Average ETF Trust (DIA). ProShares gives a warning on UDOW:

If the Index approaches a 33% loss at any point in the day, you could lose your entire investment. (...)

The performance of the Fund for periods longer than a single day will likely differ from the Daily Target. This difference may be significant.

Indeed, the daily leverage factor generates drift, a topic that I will discuss in this article.

The fund invests in instruments that, in combination, provide 3X daily leveraged exposure to the underlying index, including stocks, swaps and futures related to the index. The fund also holds cash instruments such as Treasury bills and repurchase agreements as collateral. Keeping a daily objective requires to rebalance the portfolio on a daily basis. ProShares doesn’t disclose the fund’s turnover rate on cash instruments and derivatives.

Underlying index

As described by S&P Global, eligible stocks must be part of the S&P 500 Index, excluding those classified in the Utilities sector (GICS code 55) or in the Transportation industry (GICS code 2030).

The index is a 30-stock, price-weighted index that measures the performance of some of the largest U.S. companies.

Constituents are selected by the Index Committee and changed "on an as-needed basis". There is no periodic reconstitution. The Committee doesn’t follow quantitative rules, although it considers a company’s reputation, growth, market interest, the price ratio between the highest-priced stock and the lowest-priced stock, and sector representation. The index is rebalanced semi-annually after the close of the third Friday of March and September. The turnover is low, for instance, 3% in the most recent fiscal year.

The top 10 issuers, listed in the next table based on DIA holdings, represent 54.7% of asset value and the top three names (Goldman Sachs, Caterpillar and Microsoft) weigh between 6% and 11%.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average has a focus on financials (27% of asset value) and information technology (20.6%), while other sectors are below 15%.

Compared to the S&P 500 Index, represented in the next table by SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), the Dow Jones Industrial Average is cheaper based on the P/E ratio and has lower earnings growth and cash flow growth rates.

Source: Fidelity

UDOW Portfolio

The leveraged fund has 74.8% of its asset value directly invested in the stocks of the underlying index. Swap contracts issued by six banks have an aggregate notional weight of 217.4%, and Dow Jones Industrial Average futures represent 7.8% of exposure. The total of 300% corresponds to the leverage factor.

Diversification in several swap issuers reduces counterparty risks and dependency. They are: UBS AG, SOCIÉTÉ GÉNÉRALE, BNP PARIBAS, BARCLAYS, CITIBANK, and BANK OF AMERICA.

Weights and metrics are given as an example from 11/19/2025 and may have changed by the time you read this.

Performance

UDOW has delivered 5.9 times the total return of DIA from 2/16/2010 to 11/19/2025 (through multiple splits), with a maximum drawdown of -80.3%, as reported in the table below. The ratio of annualized returns in this period is close to 2.0, below the daily leverage factor.

5.60%

Variability in the ratio points to path-dependent drift. Additionally, the Sharpe ratio of UDOW (a measure of risk-adjusted performance) is inferior to that of the unleveraged ETF on all time frames.

Why and how do leveraged ETFs drift?

Leveraged ETFs often underperform their underlying index leveraged by the same factor. ETF decay may have four reasons: beta slippage, roll yield, tracking errors, and management costs. Beta slippage is the main reason in equity leveraged ETFs such as UDOW. To understand it, imagine a very volatile asset that goes up 25% one day and down 20% the day after. A perfect 2X leveraged ETF goes up 50% the first day and down 40% the second day. At the close of the second day, the underlying asset is back to its initial price:

(1 + 0.25) x (1 - 0.2) = 1.

At the same time, the perfectly leveraged ETF has lost 10%:

(1 + 0.5) x (1 - 0.4) = 0.9.

It is just the result of compounding leveraged returns. In a trending market, beta slippage can be positive. If the underlying index goes up 10% two days in a row, on the second day, it is up 21%:

(1 + 0.1) x (1 + 0.1) = 1.21.

The perfect 2X leveraged ETFs are up 44%:

(1 + 0.2) x (1 + 0.2) = 1.44.

Beta slippage is path-dependent. If the underlying index gains 50% on day 1 and loses 33.33% on day 2, it is back to its initial value, like in the first example. However, the 2X ETF loses one-third of its value instead of 10% in the first case:

(1 + 1) x (1 - 0.6667) = 0.6667.

With this demonstration, it shows that the higher the volatility, the larger the decay.

Moreover, the higher the leverage factor, the larger the drift. If we come back to the first example of this section, a perfect 3X ETF would gain 75% on day 1 and lose 60% on day 2:

(1 + 0.75) x (1 - 0.6) = 0.7.

The 3X ETF is down 30% in two days, while the 2X ETF is down only 10% and the underlying index is unchanged.

Drift calculation and history

There is no standard definition for the drift of a leveraged ETF. Mine is simple and based on the difference between the leveraged ETF performance and Ñ times the performance of the underlying index over a given time interval, if Ñ is the leveraging factor. First, let’s start by defining “Return”: it is the total return of a leveraged ETF in a given time interval. “Index Return” is the total return of a non-leveraged ETF on the same underlying asset in the same time interval. “Abs” is the absolute value operator. “Drift” is the drift of a leveraged ETF normalized to the underlying index exposure in a time interval, calculated as follows:

Drift = (Return - (Index Return x Ñ))/ Abs(Ñ)

The next table reports monthly and annual drifts of DOW and its bear counterpart, ProShares UltraPro Short Dow30 ETF (SDOW).

Ticker

Ñ

1-month Return

1-month Drift

1-year Return

1-year Drift

DIA

0.00%

0.00%

UDOW

3

-4.74%

0.63%

-6.83%

SDOW

4.21%

0.18%

-18.49%

0.88%

UDOW has a moderate negative drift in both time frames. The next chart plots the 12-month drift of UDOW from 2/16/2011 to 11/19/2025 (to match the inception date and look-back period).

The 12-month drift has a negative historical average of -2.38%.

Related ETFs

There is no other ETF listed in the U.S. with the same underlying index and the same leverage factor. Nonetheless, the following leveraged ETFs with different factors are based on the Dow Jones Industrial Average:

  • ProShares UltraPro Short Dow30 ETF (SDOW).
  • ProShares Ultra Dow30 ETF (DDM).
  • ProShares UltraShort Dow30 ETF (DXD).
  • ProShares Short Dow30 ETF (DOG).

The next table compares their characteristics.

UDOW

SDOW

UDOW has the same expense ratio as the 2x ETF DDM and offers higher liquidity to traders.

DDM has an equivalent mutual fund, ProFunds Ultra Dow 30 Fund Investor Class (UDPIX), but the higher expense ratio of UDPIX (1.61%) makes it quite unattractive.

Takeaway: Who should consider UDOW?

UDOW is an instrument designed for traders executing strategies with clear entry and exit signals and who understand the fund’s behavior behind the leverage factor. Holding UDOW over a long period may work, but historical data underline the risk of drift and deep drawdowns. For investors willing to buy or hold UDOW, a reasonable approach is to stick to an active trading strategy backtested in all market conditions. Monitoring the drift regime and the underlying index volatility can also help decide when to adopt a risk-off stance.

This article answers these three main questions about UDOW:

  1. Is UDOW meant for short-term trading or long-term investing?
  2. Are there unique risks to investing in UDOW?
  3. What type of investor is UDOW best suited for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: BILL Holdings: Standalone Or Acquired, Investors Can Benefit From Cheap Multiples
data.description:
With the stock market appearing to take a risk-off attitude as we near the end of the year, it’s a great time for investors to rebalance our portfolios to emphasize value (and “growth at a reasonable price”) rather than double down on winning large-cap growth stocks. In particular, I’m focused on small- and mid-cap names that have their own catalysts to outperform the S&P 500.

BILL Holdings (BILL), the payments software company, is a great candidate in this regard. The stock has been pummeled this year due to macro concerns, which particularly hammers the company’s exposure to a slowdown in SMB business activity and payments. But while we certainly acknowledge there are risks to consider, the stock already prices these risks in and then some: Bill.com has crashed ~40% this year to multiyear lows.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Bill.com in August, when the stock was trading at $48 per share. Since then, Bill.com has fallen slightly further, which is opportune timing as potential M&A rumors pick up steam. The company has still managed to achieve double-digit growth in revenue and total payment volumes (TPV), leading me to reiterate my "Buy" rating on this company.

Before we dig into Bill.com’s latest fundamental trends, it’s worth commenting on the possibility of Bill.com being subject to an M&A offer. Rumors have been circulating that Bill.com has hired bankers to explore a sale, and this is a highly plausible exit scenario for the company given the very acquisitive environment in enterprise software this year. Just this week, Adobe (ADBE) purchased SEO software company Semrush (SEMR) for just shy of 4x revenue. Earlier this year, major PE firm Thoma Bravo snapped up Dayforce. These acquisitions all have a common thread with Bill.com: underappreciated valuation multiples despite strong recurring revenue.

But in my view, whether Bill.com stands to benefit from an outright sale, investors have a tremendous opportunity here to pick up the stock at a sharp discount. At current share prices near $48, Bill.com trades at a market cap of $4.87 billion. After we net off the $2.22 billion of cash and $1.87 billion of debt on the company’s latest balance sheet, Bill.com’s resulting enterprise value is $4.52 billion.

For the current year, FY26 (which ends in September 2026), Bill.com has guided to $1.59-$1.63 billion in revenue, or 9%-11% growth on 12%-15% growth in “core revenue” (which is software revenue that excludes the headwind from float interest). It’s also guiding to a midpoint 16.5% operating margin (+10bps y/y versus 16.4% margin in FY25), which is a notable increase from a previous 15.8% margin expectation and a reflection of careful expense discipline when we consider the revenue headwind from lower float interest rates.

Applying a 10bps y/y bump to Bill.com’s FCF margins would indicate a 21.5% FCF margin and $346.4 million in FCF. This positions Bill.com’s valuation multiples at:

  • 2.8x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 13.0x EV/FY27 FCF

We note that it’s incredibly rare for a company that is still growing to trade at a low teens multiple of FCF. We note as well that Bill.com is trading one turn lower than where Semrush was just acquired, and arguably Semrush had more secular headwinds working against it (AI reducing the need for web search SEO) than Bill.com’s merely cyclical interest rate and macro risks.

Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity in this stock.

Q1 download

Let’s now go through Bill.com’s latest Q1 (September quarter) results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Bill.com's revenue grew 10% y/y to $395.7 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $391.2 million (+9% y/y) by a one-point margin. Revenue from core subscriptions grew at a faster 14% y/y pace, which was largely in line with 15% y/y growth in Q2. The headwind came from net interest income, which fell 13% y/y due to lower short-term interest rates.

Despite the sharp decay in Bill.com's share price, core metrics for the business still remain quite healthy. Bill.com added 4.7k net-new customers in the quarter, bringing its total to 498.5k customers (+5% y/y)—despite the macroeconomic headwinds that tend to impact SMBs and drive small business failures to a larger degree than enterprise-oriented software companies. Also, the total payment volume processed on the Bill.com network hit a record of $89.3 billion, up 12% y/y.

Key to the company's success is a continuous rollout of new products, especially geared toward more upmarket clients, as Bill.com looks to diversify its revenue stream away from SMB. The company recently released a product called Supplier Payment Plus, geared toward the company's largest supplier customers. Though this product has longer sales cycles, it will help Bill.com build out a fuller end-to-end payments platform for more complex enterprise needs. And on the mid-market side, Bill.com notes that average card spending per customer is also rising to record highs.

Per President John Rettig's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

SPP is designed for the largest suppliers in our diversified 2-sided network and advances how we serve them by unifying payments, workflows and dedicated account management into a comprehensive purpose-built offering [...]

As with many enterprise solutions, the sales cycle is longer than our historical SMB trials, and we are building out our enterprise go-to-market capabilities to accelerate prospect conversion. Shifting to our progress in mid-market. Our Spend & Expense solution continued strong traction with higher spend businesses who typically have a large volume of transactions and complex business rules. In Q1, card spend per customer reached a record high of $145,000. In support of these larger businesses, we recently introduced a set of new functionality that enable businesses to integrate with their vendors and automatically collect, match and categorize receipts.

And on the profitability side, despite the headwinds from a decline in interest income (which represents "free" margin for the company), Bill.com still managed to slightly grow pro forma operating income to $68.2 million, or a 17.2% pro forma operating margin that is down 150bps y/y.

We note that guidance calls for just a 16.5% pro forma operating margin this year. With the latest Fed minutes potentially signaling for a slower pace of rate cuts, Bill.com may actually end up exceeding its revenue and profit guidance if rates don't drop as sharply as currently anticipated.

Key takeaways

In my view, Bill.com checks off many boxes: great valuation, reliable ~10% y/y growth in revenue, continued customer growth with an effort to land more upmarket customers, and the possibility for an M&A premium. Stay long here and utilize the dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Kanzhun: Still Positive Considering Q3 Beat And Sustained Growth
data.description:
My "Buy" rating for Kanzhun Limited (BZ) stays unchanged. I've taken into account the above-consensus 3rd quarter results and encouraging Q4 guidance in making this decision.

I touched on BZ's 2Q25 outperformance and its return of surplus capital to stockholders in the previous Aug. 22, 2025, write-up.

Quarterly Earnings Beat The Consensus Forecast

The group's financials for Jul-Sep '25 were revealed in a press release issued on Wednesday, Nov 19. BZ saw its bottom-line expansion accelerate from 2Q2025's +31% to 3Q25's +34% on a year-on-year basis.

Its third-quarter net profit amounting to CNY 0.99 billion was 6.6% above the sell-side's estimate, as per S&P Capital IQ.

BZ's strong showing was driven by both revenue improvement and profitability enhancement.

Turnover for the firm was 13% YoY higher at CNY 2.16 billion in the latest three-month period. That was better than its 2Q25 sales increase of 9%.

My late-Aug piece noted that "Kanzhun's top-line performance was previously hurt by an imbalance between the Mainland Chinese employment market's demand and supply" which "has become less of a problem." This thesis is still true.

According to BZ's results announcement, its 4Q24-3Q25 "paid enterprise customers" and 3Q2025 "average monthly active users" increased 13.3% and 10.0%, respectively, on YoY terms.

The company's analyst briefing remarks also disclosed that "the average number of daily active enterprise users grew at a faster pace sequentially than job seekers (in 3Q25), marking the first time this has happened in 3 years." This implies that the rise in prospective employees exceeded the increase in available positions on Kanzhun's platform for an extended period before the third quarter of the current year.

The favorable change in demand-supply dynamics has been good for its recent revenues.

Moving on to margins, BZ's EBIT-to-sales improved from 31.7% in 3Q24 to 41.8% for 3Q2025.

It has been managing its "Sales & Marketing/S&M" expenses well.

The 3Q25 S&M-to-turnover metric declined by 9.7ppts (percentage points) to 19.1%. In absolute terms, S&M-related costs fell 25% to CNY 0.39 billion in the quarter.

BZ explained in the earnings release that it's "benefiting from enhanced marketing efficiency driven by the platform’s strong network effects."

Anticipating A Similarly Rapid Growth Rate In Q4

The firm is guiding for sales of CNY 2,060 million (mid-point) in the final quarter of this year.

This is equivalent to a 13% increase, or the same as that for 3Q2025.

In my opinion, it can meet or even surpass the management outlook, considering key indicators presented below.

BZ's "4Q25 quarter-to-date iOS grossing was +23% yoy versus +19% yoy in 3Q25." The information was taken from Morgan Stanley's (MS) Nov. 17, 2025, research note (inaccessible in public) with the title "Chart Of The Day: iOS Grossing."

Its "In-App Purchase" or "IAP" income is growing quicker in Q4 thus far vis-a-vis Q3.

Also, Kanzhun's industry penetration rate is still reasonably modest at the low teens percent level. Its number of "paid enterprise customers" was 6.8 million for the last 12 months.

An Aug. 10, 2025, article published by Chinese state media "Global Times" stated that "as of the end of 2024, China had more than 60 million SMEs (small and medium-sized enterprises)."

Separately, BZ highlighted on the results call that it has witnessed "a potential turning point from previous downward trajectory." The group's optimism is supported by the fact that its "net dollar retention rate started to bottom out" for "the first time in the past 2 years."

Earlier, Kanzhun's corporate clients had been hesitant to extend their existing agreements relating to the former's recruitment platform solutions. Things are changing in a positive manner, as mentioned above.

You Should Consider These Worst-Case Scenarios

A supply-demand imbalance in China's jobs market will be bad for BZ. This can happen if enterprises prioritize cost-cutting and there's an influx of new university graduates in a particular academic year.

The Chinese recruitment industry's offline-to-online transition may occur at a slower-than-anticipated pace. That will affect Kanzhun's growth prospects.

My Concluding Thoughts

I'm still a bull on BZ.

I compare this name with "Recruit Holdings (OTCPK:RCRUY) (OTCPK:RCRRF) and SEEK Limited (OTCPK:SKLTY) (OTCPK:SKLTF)", as I've done in previous write-ups.

The consensus FY26 P/E multiples for BZ, RCRUY, and SKLTY are 17x, 25x, and 44x (source: S&P Capital IQ), respectively.

Kanzhun's capital appreciation potential is 47% (25/17) benchmarked with RCRRF.

I wrote in the Aug 22 update that "BZ's sales growth has moderated for six quarters in a row since Q1'24." My take at that time was "a potential reversal of this negative trend" can "act as the stock's re-rating trigger."

BZ's 4Q2025 results are expected to be released in March next year. The continuation of top-line growth in the teens percentage range should justify a more demanding valuation.
